title: Sieve array and precipitator device and method of treating exhaust
abstract: A vibrating wet precipitator is designed to remove particulates from particulate-laden hot gas. The precipitator 10 includes an array 30 of vertical wet cords 26 stretched within a duct 12. The cords 26 are tuned to vibrate due to the gas flow by controlling key parameters such as gas flow, velocity, cord length and diameter so that particulate collection and heat transfer efficiency are maximized. The cords are part of sieves 24. A plurality of these sieves are arranged to define a plurality of gaps 28, through which the exhaust flows. The sieves 24 and thus the cords 26 are spaced so that a vortex from a first cord 26(a) affects an adjacent cord 26(b) and a subsequent cord 26(c). The particles are then absorbed in liquid, which can be passed through a heat exchanger, filtered and subsequently reused. Preferably the cord arrangement is designed to allow the cords to vibrate at high frequencies, typically 10 to 100 Hz, to maximize particulate collection.
claims: Claims What is claimed is: 1 . A precipitator device for reducing particulate and gaseous emissions from an exhaust, comprising; a flow chamber defining a flow path therethrough; a plurality of sieves arranged relative to each other to define a plurality of gaps therebetween for receiving the exhaust therethrough, each of the sieves including a plurality of elongated cords longitudinally extending therealong and configured to transversely vibrate between a first position and a second position such that each of the elongated cords generates a trailing vortex within the exhaust for collecting a plurality of particulates within the trailing vortex. 2. The precipitator device of claim 1 , wherein each of the plurality of sieves includes an inlet for receiving a liquid and an outlet for discharging the liquid and further comprises: a liquid collector positioned proximate to the outlet of each of the plurality of sieves and configured to collect the liquid therefrom. 3. The precipitator device of claim 2, further comprising: a heat exchanger fluidly connected to the liquid collector and configured to receive the liquid from the liquid collector after having been heated by the exhaust to recover a thermal energy therefrom. 4. The precipitator device of claim 2, wherein the plurality of sieves are configured to generate a condensate from the exhaust and the liquid and the condensate flow together into the liquid collector, and the precipitator device further comprises: a pump fluidly connected to the liquid collector and the inlet of the plurality of sieves such that the pump directs the liquid and the condensate from the liquid collector to the inlet for reuse. 5. The precipitator device of claim 1 , wherein each of the elongated cords is exposed to the flow path within the flow chamber such that the exhaust flowing therethrough directly contacts the liquid flowing along the cord. 6. The precipitator device of claim 1 , wherein the plurality of sieves is electrically grounded and further comprises: a plurality of discharge electrodes positioned proximate to the plurality of sieves and electrically connected to a current supply such that the plurality of discharge electrodes charge a plurality of particulates with a plurality of charged particles flowing with the exhaust for accumulating the charged plurality of particulates on the plurality of sieves. 7. The precipitator device of claim 1 , wherein the plurality of sieves extend perpendicular to the flow path within the flow chamber, and are positioned so that the trailing vortex of a first sieve affects an adjacent sieve. 8. A sieve assembly for treating an exhaust, comprising; a plurality of sieves arranged relative to each other to define a plurality of gaps therebetween for receiving the exhaust therethrough, each of the sieves including an elongated cord longitudinally extending therealong and configured to transversely vibrate between a first position and a second position such that each of the elongated cords generates a trailing vortex within the exhaust for collecting a plurality of particulates within the trailing vortex. 9. The sieve assembly of claim 8, further comprising: a first support member and an opposing second support member, the plurality of sieves connected to and extending between the first and second support members such that each of the elongated cords is held in tension therebetween. 10. The sieve assembly of claim 9, wherein each of the elongated cords is held in a predetermined tension such that each of the elongated cords is configured to vibrate by directing a flow of the exhaust transversely therealong. 1 1. The sieve assembly of claim 8, further comprising: a first support member defining a liquid supply conduit configured to receive the liquid, the plurality of sieves projecting from the support member such that each of the elongated cords is fluidly connected to the liquid supply conduit to receive the liquid and pass liquid therealong as each of the elongated cords vibrates. 12. The sieve assembly claimed in claim 1 1 further comprising a liquid collector connected to a heat exchanger. 13. The sieve assembly of claim 1 1 , wherein each of the elongated cords are formed from a hydrophobic material. 14. The sieve assembly of claim 8, wherein each of the elongated cords is formed from a wire rope. 15. A method of treating an exhaust with a plurality of sieves arranged relative to each other to define a plurality of gaps therebetween for receiving the exhaust therethrough, each of the sieves including an elongated cord longitudinally extending therealong and configured to transversely vibrate between a first position and a second position, the method comprising: flowing a liquid along the elongated cords and impacting the plurality of particulates against the liquid to further remove the plurality of particulates from the flow of the exhaust. directing the exhaust toward the plurality of sieves; vibrating each of the elongated cords in order to respectively generate a plurality of trailing vortices within a flow of the exhaust, each trailing vortex respectively following the vibrational transverse movement of each of the elongated cords; impacting a plurality of particulates from the exhaust against each of the elongated cords in order to remove the plurality of large particulates from the flow of the exhaust and thereby treating the exhaust. 16. The method of claim 15, wherein the plurality of particulates includes a plurality of small particulates, and the method further comprises: collecting a plurality of small particulates of the exhaust within the plurality of trailing vortices; coalescing the plurality of small particulates within the trailing vortices into a plurality of large particulates; and impacting the plurality of large particulates from the exhaust against each of the elongated cords. 17. The method of claim 15, wherein said elongated cord are vibrated at least at a frequency of 10-100 Hertz. 18. The method of claim 17, wherein each of the elongated cords is formed from a liquid permeable material, and the method further comprises: permeating the liquid along the liquid permeable material such that the liquid flows along the liquid permeable material. 19. The method of claim 17, further comprising: scatter the liquid from each of the elongated cords in the form of a plurality of liquid droplets; and amalgamating the plurality of particulates with the plurality of liquid droplets to further treat the exhaust. 20. The method of claim 15, further comprising: directing the flow of the exhaust transversely along each of the elongated cords in order to cause each of the elongated cords to vibrate. 21 . The method of claim 15, further comprising: electrically grounding the plurality of sieves; electrically powering a plurality of discharge electrodes proximate to the plurality of sieves; electrically charging the plurality of particulates with a plurality of particles within the exhaust; and accumulating the charged plurality of particulates on the plurality of sieves. 22. A precipitator device for reducing particulate and gaseous emissions from an exhaust comprising: a flow chamber defining a flow path therethrough having a horizontal portion and a vertical portion; a plurality of sieves in said vertical portion, said sieves arranged relative to each other to define a plurality of gaps therebetween for receiving the exhaust therethrough; each of said sieves including an elongated cord extending from a first side to a second side of said vertical portion and arranged in a slanted position relative to the flow said gas; a first end of said cord connected to a water supply; a water collection trough located near a second end of said cords effective to collect water dripping from said cords; said trough having drain numbers effective to direct water collected in said trough downwardly across said horizontal portion of said flow chamber into a water collection device. 23. The precipitator device claimed in claim 22 further comprising a recirculation flow path directing water from said water collection trough to said water source. 24. The precipitator device claimed in claim 23 further comprising a heat exchanger fluidly connected to said recirculation flow path. 25. The apparatus claimed in claim 1 wherein said cords each have lengths and said lengths of said cords are established to increase frequencies of vibrations of said cords. 26. The method claimed in claim 15 wherein lengths of said elongated cords are established to increase a frequency of said elongated cords.
